text: Finance teams are heavy in like the Philippines. Yeah, 100%. And you have, and you need the same issues there, right? They have expenses, employees, reimbursements, benefits, like all that stuff. And we need a platform to deal with it. So that became a very obvious thing that most companies are becoming global sooner. And then on the partnership side, the other thing we realized on the enterprise is you can't build everything yourself. because customers have very different, and there's this belief that, you know, people talk about the office of the CFO, but the reality is the office of the CFO doesn't exist. There's no CFO in an ivory tower making buying decisions saying, you know what, now we're going to buy. You mean these make up terms for categories? Yeah, it does exist in the office of the CRO because CRO actually has an ivory tower called RevOps, and RevOps makes a lot of revenue decisions, and they say, we're going to buy Salesforce, we're going to buy Gong, we're going to buy this and that, and actually aggregate these decisions under one umbrella. Not true in the financing. Financing makes decisions totally separately. A person managing procurement doesn't know what's happening in FB&A necessarily, doesn't know necessarily what's happening on accounting and which ERP is being used, doesn't necessarily know what's happening on treasury and cards. So all of these things are done in isolation from one another. So the learning that we had there is when you go into an enterprise company, they already have a set of systems in place. and these systems need to do well and play well of cards. So instead of actually trying to go in and say, you know what, if you want to buy Brex, you have to replace your travel system. You have to replace your procurement system. I think it would be great to tell a customer story. You don't necessarily even have to name it, but Brex has done a partnership with Navon. Brex has travel expense management. Navon is a dedicated expense management. And from the outside, you look at it and you're like, aren't you competitors? So walk through an example of why that partnership makes sense. A very clear example is you get a large public company, probably a hundred billion dollar company at this point. Who is a Brex customer. Brex customer. And they also use Navon for travel. And they say you know what I have Navon for travel Navon has you know there literally a card on file to pay for trips right So the first thing is that card being a Brex card makes sense right Because you already used both products. And then what we've done is we've said, hey, if you integrate Brex and Navon together, we create this whole degree of experience. We actually have a video of a customer crying when they saw this for the first time, where we literally... You can send it to us afterwards. We can send it and we can maybe add it to your podcast. And the customer literally say, I don't believe this. I never saw this before because 100% of the travel spend gets reconciled automatically. 100%, every dollar. They literally do not have to do any accounting because Brex allows them to have literally a single virtual card for every trip, for every hotel booking. And we can automatically reconcile that to the right jail, the right department, the right trip, the right customer, the right everything on your accounting. And there's no work for you, right? So a lot of these partnerships started by saying, well, how do we make a one plus one equals three scenario where you bring two products together and actually make a great experience? So Zip was similar. Zip was like, you know, we have a lot of customers that have really complex procurement flows. And they say, you know what, I want to use actually a modern solution. Zip built a lot of that and serves a lot of companies. And we said, why don't we bring both together and build a perfect purchasing experience where, you know, you actually have virtual card provisioning. You have contracts that have reviews. all of that integrated into a single experience. And then the benefit is we can actually go to both Zip and Avon and offer them all the capabilities that our customers have. So that all works globally. We do all the underwriting, all the risk management, all the back office of handling cards, settlement payments, treasury, etc., capital markets. And then they get to focus on the workflow that they do best. We get to focus on all the financial infrastructure. and it becomes a win-win for the customer because they have the best of both worlds and the entire ecosystem plays well on the enterprise. And you can't just really go and assume that a single company will do it all given the sheer complexity of these Fortune 500 companies and much larger scale companies. Yeah, I think it's very hard for external observers to intuit why exactly because these things seem maybe competitive outside but if you actually talking to enterprise customers it a very different world Exactly And you know and I think we been very fortunate to have great partners And, you know, and I think what we saw is, like, we're stronger together than competing. And I think it took us years to build a maturity on partnerships in order to, you know, realize that, you know, some companies have competition on the bounds in the products they operate. But the reality at the end of the day is if you're interested in serving this specific customer really well, there are moments that coming together is actually much better for the customer. Yeah. And so there's kind of the three pillars of Brex 3.0 would be partnerships, global, and then AI. That's right. At its core. I'm also curious, back up to like AI at its core stuff, and building a team, you're an ex-engineer or current engineer. Like, are you pushing code to production currently on the new product, or do you? I do push some. Not as aggressively as I would like because I have a day job. But in the evenings and weekends, I definitely push some code. I think it's very important to remain technical. And I certainly spend a lot of time trying out the new tools, especially on developer productivity and things build on the bounds of products you build. But, yeah, I think remaining technical is very important. Why do you think it is important to remain technical when you're at CIO? Like, what insights does it give you? Well, I think now we completely changed the way and the bar for building products. And if you don't have a very good intuitive understanding of the limitations and where the bounds are to technology, your ability to plan, to understand where the world's going, to think about the product, and also just use tools that have good taste in the AI decisions they've made, I think is highly limiting. So I think it's really hard to be at the forefront of the product building if you're not at the forefront of the technology and really understanding what's actually happening. And not just on an engineering level, but also on the AI and research level. So a huge benefit of being in the Bay Area is you got to spend a lot of time with researchers in the big AI labs You got to spend a lot of time with investors that are really understanding what happening at the bleeding edge And for better or for worse, that gives you more foresight and visibility into where the world's going. Yeah, and we're doing this interview today from your SF HQ. How do you feel like San Francisco as an ecosystem has changed in the last even six months? I mean, AI saved us half in some ways, right? So I think that's been awesome to see. I mean, we're very bullish on the Bay Area in general. And I think the best way to think about it is, you know, I don't think there's been, you know, someone, one of our investors told me that, you know, there hasn't been, pre-AI actually, this was in 2022, there hasn't been this amount of wealth creation in a 50-mile radius since the Roman Empire. Just percentage of wealth creation inside a circle versus outside, which is insane. And I think now is going to be much more true than before. And I think that's a special statement. And I think actually the most special part to me is just the talent and what that means in terms of the kinds of people that exist. Yeah. And I think there's something special about being able to be part of the ecosystem here. We're pretty bullish on a lot of the things happening on the ground, not just on the sort of larger companies' labs, et cetera, but also like smaller companies. Like we have a lot of customers that are actually building really amazing things, even though they're just starting out. And we just see the rate and the quality of the products early on becoming materially higher than before. So there's a lot of good progress there, and overall we're pretty bullish. Yeah, it's an amazing time to be in San Francisco. Like, this building specifically, you guys are here. It's 83rd 2nd Street, I think is the address. Used to be the Plaid headquarters. You can go down to Mission Bay, and it's like Uber's headquarters, which was the biggest success story for a long time. It's right next to, like, the OpenAI headquarters. It's just incredible to be able to walk around. 100%. Awesome. Well, thanks so much for doing this podcast. I'm excited to follow what you guys ship next. Thanks for having me.
